A **Jewish quota** is a discriminatory [racial quota](/wiki/Racial_quota "Racial quota") designed to limit or deny access for [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") to various institutions. Such quotas were widespread in the 19th and 20th centuries in developed countries and frequently present in [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education"), often at prestigious universities.

## By countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: By countries")]

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Canada")]

Some universities in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), notably [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University"), the [Université de Montréal](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Université de Montréal") and the [University of Toronto Faculty of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Toronto_Faculty_of_Medicine "University of Toronto Faculty of Medicine"), had longstanding quotas on the number of Jews admitted to the respective universities. McGill University’s strict quota was the longest-running, having been officially adopted in 1920 and remaining in place until the late 1960s.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: France")]

[Vichy France](/wiki/Vichy_France "Vichy France") established a [Commissariat-General for Jewish Affairs](/wiki/Commissariat-General_for_Jewish_Affairs "Commissariat-General for Jewish Affairs") led by the French antisemite and Nazi collaborator [Xavier Vallat](/wiki/Xavier_Vallat "Xavier Vallat"). It published the [second law on the status of Jews](/wiki/Second_law_on_the_status_of_Jews "Second law on the status of Jews") on June 2, 1941, which sought to exclude Jews from economic life.[[2]](#cite_note-:22-2) A [numerus clausus](/wiki/Numerus_clausus "Numerus clausus") came into effect in the liberal professions of medicine, pharmacy, and the bar, as well as at universities and *[grandes écoles](/wiki/Grande_%C3%A9cole "Grande école")*.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3): 145

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Germany")]

Further information (in German): [Law against overcrowding in German schools and universities](/w/index.php?title=Law_against_overcrowding_in_German_schools_and_universities&action=edit&redlink=1 "Law against overcrowding in German schools and universities (page does not exist)")[[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gesetz_gegen_die_%C3%9Cberf%C3%BCllung_deutscher_Schulen_und_Hochschulen "de:Gesetz gegen die Überfüllung deutscher Schulen und Hochschulen")]

In Germany, a whole series of *[numerus clausus](/wiki/Numerus_clausus "Numerus clausus")* resolutions were adopted in 1929 on the basis of race and place of origin, not religion.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On 25 April 1933, the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") government introduced a 1.5% quota for new admissions of German non-[Aryans](/wiki/Aryan_race "Aryan race")—ie., essentially of German Jews—as core issue of a law claiming to generally limit the number of (Aryan and non-Aryan) students admitted to high-schools (*höhere Schulen*) and universities. In addition, high-schools and universities deemed to have more students than required for the professions for which they were training their students were required to reduce their student enrollment; in doing so, they had to reach a maximum of 5% of German non-Aryan students. The law was supposedly enacted to avoid overcrowding schools and universities,[[5]](#cite_note-5) which cited apparent concerns at the time that large numbers of students would decrease the quality of higher education in Germany. At the beginning of 1933, about 0.76% of the German population was Jewish, but more than 3.6% of German university students were Jewish, this number having steadily declined from over 9% in the 1880s.[[6]](#cite_note-6) After 30 July 1939, Jews were no longer permitted to attend German public schools at all, and the prior quota law was eliminated by a non-public regulation in January 1940.[[7]](#cite_note-Olenhusen-7)p. 193

In addition to their strong and predominantly antisemitic agenda, the law and subsequent regulations were temporarily used to limit general university access to other groups that were not deemed "non-Aryan", as the name of the law implied. Starting in 1934, a regulation limited the overall numbers of students admitted to German universities, and a special quota was introduced reducing women's admissions to a maximum of 10%. Although the limits were not entirely enforced, the women's quota stayed a bit above 10% mainly because a smaller percentage of men than women accepted their university admissions, which made it approximately twice as hard for women to enter a university career than for men with the same qualification.[[8]](#cite_note-Bildungsbürgerinnen-8)S. 80ff. After two semesters, these admission limits were revoked, however, leaving in place the non-Aryan regulations.[[7]](#cite_note-Olenhusen-7)p. 178

### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hungary")]

The *Numerus Clausus* Act was introduced in 1920, under the government of [Pál Teleki](/wiki/P%C3%A1l_Teleki "Pál Teleki"). It was said that the ethnic makeup of student bodies must meet the ethnic rate of population, because Jews made up roughly 6% of the population, the policy effectively limited Jewish students to about 6% of university places, sharply reducing their previous representation of around 15%.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The law was actively enforced by university administrators and nationalist student groups, who often harassed Jewish students, leading many Jews to pursue studies abroad.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Limitations were relaxed in 1928 due to international pressure, namely the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"). Racial criteria in admitting new students were removed and replaced by social criteria. Five categories were set up: civil servants, war veterans and army officers, small landowners and artisans, industrialists, and the merchant classes.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Poland")]

See [Numerus clausus in Poland](/wiki/Numerus_clausus#Poland "Numerus clausus") and [Ghetto benches](/wiki/Ghetto_benches "Ghetto benches").

### Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Romania")]

*Numerus Clausus* was not introduced by law,[[14]](#cite_note-14) but it was adopted by students in the universities Cluj, Bucharest, Iasi and Cernauti.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Russia")]

*Numerus Clausus* was enacted in 1887, stating that the share of Jewish students should be no more than 10 percent in cities where [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") were [allowed to live](/wiki/Pale_of_Settlement "Pale of Settlement"), 5 percent in other cities, and only 3 percent in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") and [St. Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"). As well, there were quotas for Jewish and other non-Orthodox or non-Russian persons in joining the army.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) These limitations were removed in the spring of 1917 after the tsar's abdication during the early phase of the Russian revolution of 1917–1918 (the so-called [February Revolution](/wiki/February_Revolution "February Revolution") of 1917); later, in the late 1940s, during the initial phase of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and the tide of the anti-"[rootless cosmopolitan](/wiki/Rootless_cosmopolitan "Rootless cosmopolitan")" campaign, a [de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto") gross discrimination of Jewish applicants was reintroduced in many institutions of higher education in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") until [Perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: United States")]

See also: [Numerus clausus § North America](/wiki/Numerus_clausus#North_America "Numerus clausus")

Certain private universities, most notably [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard"), introduced policies which effectively placed a quota on the number of Jews admitted to the university.[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Abbott Lawrence Lowell](/wiki/A._Lawrence_Lowell "A. Lawrence Lowell"), the president of Harvard University from 1909 to 1933,[[22]](#cite_note-22) raised the alarm about a ‘[Jewish problem](/wiki/Jewish_problem "Jewish problem")’ when the number of Jewish students grew from six percent to twenty-two percent between 1908 and 1922.[[23]](#cite_note-auto-23) Lowell argued that a "limit be placed on the number of them who later be admitted to the university."[[23]](#cite_note-auto-23) The implementation of a quota on the number of Jews was not unique to Harvard. After Harvard’s 1926 announcement about instating a "new admissions policy [that] would place great emphasis on character and personality, the *Yale Daily News* praised its decision and put forward its own version of how Yale should select its students in a major editorial, ‘[Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") for Yale.’ It called on the university to institute immigration laws more prohibitive than those of the United States government."[[24]](#cite_note-24)

According to historian [David Oshinsky](/wiki/David_Oshinsky "David Oshinsky"), writing about [Jonas Salk](/wiki/Jonas_Salk "Jonas Salk"), "Most of the surrounding medical schools ([Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"), and [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")) had rigid quotas in place. In 1935 Yale accepted 76 applicants from a pool of 501. About 200 of those applicants were Jewish and only five got in." He notes that Dean Milton Winternitz's instructions were remarkably precise: "Never admit more than five Jews, take only two Italian Catholics, and take no blacks at all."[[25]](#cite_note-25) As a result, Oshinsky added, "[Jonas Salk](/wiki/Jonas_Salk "Jonas Salk") and hundreds like him" enrolled in [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") instead.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Physicist and Nobel laureate [Richard P. Feynman](/wiki/Richard_P._Feynman "Richard P. Feynman") was turned away from [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College_of_Columbia_University "Columbia College of Columbia University") in the 1930s and went to [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") instead.

According to Dan Oren's book, *Joining the Club — A History of Jews and Yale*, Yale University's informal admissions policy to restrict the school's Jewish student body to around 10 percent ended in the early 1960s.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Yugoslavia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_quota&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Yugoslavia")]

In 1940, the government of the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") enacted the Decree on the Enrollment of Persons of Jewish Descent at the University, Secondary School, Teacher Training College and Other Vocational Schools which limited the proportion of Jewish students to the proportion of Jews in the total population.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
